macro_summary: Tuesday's session saw the S&P 500 climb 0.84% to a fresh record 723.77, with the Nasdaq-100 up 1.43% as tech earnings continued to impress—though those gains were pre-released after the close (AMD, LITE). The VIX fell 4.92% to 17.38, and the US10Y eased 0.67% to 4.42%. Oil steadied at $100.61 after Monday's surge, while the FOMC decision tomorrow now becomes the primary macro driver.
commodities_note: WTI at $100.61 (+0.16%) consolidated below the $105 spike; gold was flat at $4,658. The Hormuz-driven energy premium persists but US naval escort proposals and a 'pause' from Trump eased immediate supply fears. Gold remains near all-time highs as a geopolitical hedge.

singapore.summary: The Singapore bank cluster remains on hold as NIM compression persists with US10Y at 4.42% and MAS S$NEER unchanged. OCBC reports May 8 and UOB May 7 — these prints will determine whether the NIM trough is forming. DBS is the only name technically blocked (RSI 69.7).
singapore.key_development: OCBC's acquisition of HSBC Indonesia retail/wealth units (announced May 5) adds scale but doesn't address the core NIM headwind.

semiconductors.summary: Semiconductor stocks rallied sharply Tuesday (SOXX +4.72% to 482.73) on AMD's pre-release beat and AI memory demand, but almost every name is now mechanically blocked by extreme extension. The cluster is structurally bullish (AI capex confirmed) but tactically extended. Only ASML and NVDA have re-entered watchable zones; the rest require pullbacks of 20%+ before entry is viable.
semiconductors.key_development: AMD's Q1 beat and Q2 guidance (released after close) lifted the entire semis complex, but the extreme runs in MU, INTC, SK Hynix, and LITE preclude any fresh entry.

big_tech.summary: Big Tech is similarly extended: AMZN and GOOG sit at 20%+ above their 50-day SMAs, hard-blocked. MSFT is the sole hold, with no compelling entry trigger. PLTR fell further post-earnings, now below its 50-day SMA, but its 68x PE keeps it in no-man's land.
big_tech.thesis_status: Mixed — capex confirmed, but credit-circularity risk remains
big_tech.key_development: Morgan Stanley's $805B Big Tech capex forecast for 2026 drives the bull case, but the stocks are already pricing that in; new cash has no attractive entry point.

interconnected.name: Coherent Corp.
interconnected.reason: NVIDIA's $2B photonics investment partner (alongside LITE) — the AI optical interconnect buildout directly benefits Coherent's transceiver and laser business.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if LITE's post-earnings pullback converges with COHR's own pullback to its 200-day SMA (~$85), or after NVIDIA's May 21 earnings confirm photonics acceleration.

interconnected.name: Marvell Technology
interconnected.reason: Marvell's custom ASIC and data centre networking silicon ties to the same hyperscaler capex cycle as AVGO and NVDA — a less-extended alternative if semis pull back.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if MRVL pulls back 8-10% from current levels (around $85) and reports a new cloud ASIC win at its next earnings (date TBD).

active_risks: Iran-Hormuz conflict: oil shipping lanes partially disrupted for 58 days; US naval escort proposal paused, but missile activity persists. WTI at $100.61.
US-China tech trade war: new tariffs and sanctions escalate, reshaping semiconductor supply chains. ASML, INTC, MU face bifurcation risk.
Fed policy uncertainty: FOMC decision tomorrow (May 7) with US10Y at 4.42% — rate cut timeline pushed to late 2026 on sticky inflation from oil.
Singapore bank NIM compression: dual headwinds from elevated US rates and MAS S$NEER policy persist; no relief expected until October 2026 at earliest.
geopolitical.market_impact: Oil at $100.61 keeps energy costs elevated, but the 'Trump pause' on Hormuz intervention relieved immediate supply fears, allowing risk assets to rally. Semis (SOXX +4.72%) largely ignored the oil risk, focusing on AI earnings. Singapore banks are stuck with no NIM relief — OCBC and UOB earnings this week will be the first test of whether asset quality offsets margin pressure.
geopolitical.new_since_yesterday: US President Trump 'paused' his Hormuz opening effort after missile activity; the JOLTS report showed March job openings falling to 6.9M (also released May 5). No new FOMC signals ahead of tomorrow's decision.
geopolitical.portfolio_action: Hold fire on all new entries until the FOMC decision and OCBC/UOB earnings resolve. The semis surge is not entry-able given extreme extensions.

portfolio_snapshot.overall_stance: Defensive — extreme extensions block new entry across semis and Big Tech; only three names are watchable.
portfolio_snapshot.risk_posture: Fifteen of 21 names are hard-blocked on technical extension (RSI≥65 or >20% above SMA50). The FOMC tomorrow and a cluster of earnings (UOB, OCBC, CoreWeave) pose binary risks that argue for patience. No new capital deployment until the post-FOMC and post-earnings landscape clarifies.
